description: Symptom: Excessive connection lost and connection established logging when multiple NTP servers are configured. Scenario: If the NTP server changes, a connection lost and connection established is logged to the event log. Workaround: The fix disables logging of normal server changes and only logs events if server reachability reaches 0 or NTP synchronization is lost.

description: Symptom: Insufficient argument validation could cause a system crash when executing certain ovs-appctl mirror commands with incomplete parameters. Scenario: Executing mirror management commands with an improper number of arguments could cause memory access violations, potentially resulting in a switch crash. Workaround: Commands now verify that the required number of arguments is provided before processing operations. When insufficient arguments are detected, error messages are displayed with usage guidance.

description: Symptom: An STP port incorrectly transitions into Disabled/Blocked state. Scenario: During certain port flap events, STP may erroneously mark a port as Disabled/Blocked, even when the link should remain active. Workaround: Shut down all affected ports. Restart the hpe-pvstd process using the following commands: start-shell sudo bash ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep systemctl restart hpe-pvstd ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep exit exit

description: Symptom: VM-to-VM communication fails across the VXLAN tunnel between two sites. Packet captures show outbound traffic from 10000 switches contains unexpected inner VLAN tags. Destination VMs receive frames with double VLAN tagging (outer VLAN + inner VLAN). Network connectivity is broken for VMs in VXLAN. Traffic that should be flowing seamlessly over the VXLAN overlay is dropped. Scenario: The VSX pair in each rack is configured to extend VLANs over VXLAN in a back-to-back leaf topology without spine switches. Host-facing ports use persona access configuration while inter-site links use persona uplink. When traffic originates from VMs and traverses the VXLAN tunnel between sites, the 10K switches across tunnel are failing to remove the inner VLAN tag. This results in the destination VM receiving packets with an unexpected inner VLAN tag, causing them to drop the packets, breaking end-to-end connectivity.

messages.content: For 10000 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, for 10,000 AOS-CX 10.16.1010 devices, how do you perform the software upgrade?

Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the 'boot system <BOOT-BANK>' command and enter 'n' when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, ensure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the 'allow non-failsafe updates' command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the 'boot system <BOOT-BANK>' command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

description: Symptom: VM-to-VM communication fails across the VXLAN tunnel between two sites. Packet captures show outbound traffic from 10000 switches contains unexpected inner VLAN tags. Destination VMs receive frames with double VLAN tagging (outer VLAN + inner VLAN). Network connectivity is broken for VMs in VXLAN. Traffic that should be flowing seamlessly over the VXLAN overlay is dropped. Scenario: The VSX pair in each rack is configured to extend VLANs over VXLAN in a back-to-back leaf topology without spine switches. Host-facing ports use persona access configuration while inter-site links use persona uplink. When traffic originates from VMs and traverses the VXLAN tunnel between sites, the 10K switches across tunnel are failing to remove the inner VLAN tag. This results in the destination VM receiving packets with an unexpected inner VLAN tag, causing them to drop the packets, breaking end-to-end connectivity.
